region_of_origin: Global (widely cultivated; Granny Smith variety originated in Australia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers apple as a whole food item; green variety is a colour/cultivar sub-type not separately coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00FL maps to apple (generic); green/Granny Smith cultivar distinction is not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium green apple (approximately 150 g) is a typical single serving, providing about 72 kcal and 16.2 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Apple, raw, with skin — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for green apple (raw, with skin). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this record with confidence 0.95 as indicated in the input per_100g_meta.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods — Apple (raw, green/Granny Smith)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 36 (low) for green apple is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for raw green apple (Granny Smith type). Glycemic load of 3.9 per 100g serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (36 × 10.8) / 100 = 3.9. This is classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100g), moderate potassium (99 mg/100g), and 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100g support blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Green apple is suitable for individuals with hypertension; consume whole with skin to maximise potassium and fibre intake, and avoid apple juice which removes the fibre that moderates sugar absorption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 36) and 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100g slow glucose absorption; intrinsic fructose is metabolised more slowly than glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Green apple is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (1 medium apple per serving); pairing with a protein source such as nut butter further attenuates the glycaemic response, and individual blood glucose monitoring is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0 g/100g confirmed), zero cholesterol, low sodium, and 2.4 g soluble and insoluble dietary fibre per 100g support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green apple is a heart-friendly fruit choice; its dietary fibre contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction, its negligible sodium and fat content align with cardiovascular dietary guidelines, and regular whole-fruit consumption is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk in epidemiological studies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Green apple provides quercetin and vitamin C with anti-inflammatory properties, but does not meet the threshold of positive anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, >3g whole-food fibre, cruciferous vegetables, anti-inflammatory spices) required to classify it as actively beneficial for RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Green apple may be consumed as part of a varied anti-inflammatory diet for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; eat with skin for maximum quercetin and fibre content, and choose organic where possible to reduce pesticide exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green apple (Apple, green) is a fresh, raw fruit characterised by its bright green skin, crisp white flesh, and distinctively tart-to-mildly-sweet flavour profile. The most widely recognised green apple cultivar is the Granny Smith, which originated in Australia and is now cultivated globally. Green apples are consumed raw as a snack, sliced in salads, or used in cooking and baking where their firm texture and acidity are valued. Per 100g, green apple provides 48 kcal, 0.3g protein, 0g fat, 10.8g carbohydrate (of which 10.4g is sugar), 2.4g dietary fibre, and only 2mg sodium. Its low glycemic index (GI 36, classified as low) and moderate fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension. The negligible fat and cholesterol content, combined with dietary fibre that supports LDL reduction, make it a heart-friendly food. Green apple contains quercetin and vitamin C, polyphenol antioxidants with anti-inflammatory properties. It is naturally plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is commonly consumed as a snack and is classified as a low-calorie, low-sodium, plant-based food. Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database.
common_user_queries: Is green apple good for diabetes?
How many calories in a green apple?
Green apple vs red apple nutrition
Can I eat green apple if I have high blood pressure?
Is green apple good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in input per_100g_meta. GI value of 36 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published values for green apple. All core macros and secondary nutrients are supported by named approved sources.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database for raw green apple; this is a naturally fat-free fruit. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant food containing no cholesterol by nature. Saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source record for this entry; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09003, Apples, raw, with skin) reports vitamin C ~4.6mg/100g, vitamin A ~3mcg RAE/100g, and water ~85.6g/100g as reference values but these are not substituted here to preserve source integrity. GL per serving calculated as (36 × 10.8) / 100 = 3.9 based on 100g reference portion. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable=false but the reason text described anti-inflammatory properties; this has been corrected to suitable=true with a moderate concern level reflecting that green apple provides some anti-inflammatory phytonutrients but does not meet the full threshold for active RA benefit. The high_sugar_warning dietary tag in the input refers to total sugar content of 10.4g/100g; this is intrinsic fruit sugar accompanied by fibre and does not trigger the AHA added-sugar gate applicable to processed foods and sugar-sweetened beverages.
